perturbation.original_text: Impresse shall use commercially reasonable efforts to respond to all support requests by VerticalNet relating to the Co-Branded Site within one Business Day (as defined below) of Impresse's receipt of such notification. Impresse shall use reasonable efforts to cure the reported problem as soon as reasonably possible.
perturbation.changed_text: Impresse will endeavor to address support requests from VerticalNet relating to the Co-Branded Site when feasible. Impresse will attempt to resolve any reported problem in a convenient timeframe.
perturbation.explanation: The original text sets a clear standard of 'commercially reasonable efforts' and a specific timeframe (one business day). The modified text weakens this obligation by replacing 'shall use commercially reasonable efforts' with 'will endeavor to address,' which implies a lesser commitment. Additionally, 'reasonable efforts to cure as soon as reasonably possible' is replaced with 'attempt to resolve any reported problem in a convenient timeframe,' removing any sense of urgency or defined resolution target. This change creates ambiguity in the level of support and time frame for resolution.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code § 17500 prohibits false or misleading advertising. By weakening the obligation to provide timely and effective support, the modified text could be interpreted as misleading if Impresse advertises or implies a higher level of support than it actually provides. The 'convenient timeframe' clause introduces a level of subjectivity that could allow Impresse to delay or neglect support requests, potentially harming VerticalNet's business and giving a false impression of the co-branded site's reliability.

Advertising [17500 - 17606] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 63. ) ARTICLE 1. False Advertising in General [17500 - 17509] ( Article 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 63. ) 17500. It is unlawful for any person, firm, corporation or association, or any employee thereof with intent directly or indirectly to dispose of real or personal property or to perform services, professional or otherwise, or anything of any nature whatsoever or to induce the public to enter into any obligation relating thereto, to make or disseminate or cause to be made or disseminated before the public in this state, or to make or disseminate or cause to be made or disseminated from this state before the public in any state, in any newspaper or other publication, or any advertising device, or by public outcry or proclamation, or in any other manner or means whatever, including over the Internet, any statement, concerning that real or personal property or those services, professional or otherwise, or concerning any circumstance or matter of fact connected with the propo
perturbation.justification: The changed text weakens the original contract's obligation to provide timely and effective support, which could be interpreted as misleading advertising under California Business and Professions Code § 17500. The original text specifies 'commercially reasonable efforts' and 'one Business Day,' while the changed text uses vaguer terms like 'will endeavor to address' and 'convenient timeframe.' This ambiguity allows Impresse to delay or neglect support requests, giving a false impression of the co-branded site's reliability. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing BPC 17500, states that it is 'unlawful' to make or disseminate any statement concerning services to induce the public into any obligation which could be argued here, due to the weakening of obligation to support.

perturbation.original_text: Each party shall comply with all prevailing laws, rules and regulations and obtain all necessary approvals, consents and permits required by the applicable agencies of the government of the jurisdictions that apply to its activities or obligations under this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party shall endeavor, to the extent practicable, to generally adhere to applicable laws, rules, and regulations, and will seek necessary approvals when convenient.
perturbation.explanation: The original text creates a firm obligation to comply with all applicable laws. The modified text weakens this obligation by replacing 'shall comply' with 'shall endeavor, to the extent practicable, to generally adhere to,' making compliance subject to feasibility and interpretation. Furthermore, changing 'obtain all necessary approvals, consents and permits' to 'will seek necessary approvals when convenient' introduces a discretionary element, potentially leading to non-compliance with regulatory requirements. The addition of the word 'generally' means that they do not have to comply with *all* laws, rules, and regulations.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Corporations Code § 201 specifies the powers of a California corporation, which implicitly includes the obligation to operate within the bounds of the law. By making legal compliance discretionary ('to the extent practicable,' 'generally adhere,' 'when convenient'), the modified text suggests that Impresse, a California corporation, might not fully commit to fulfilling this fundamental obligation. This introduces ambiguity about the extent to which Impresse intends to abide by California law, potentially undermining the legal basis of the agreement itself and raising questions about its adherence to mandatory corporate responsibilities.

CORPORATIONS [100 - 14707] ( Title 1 enacted by Stats. 1947, Ch. 1038. ) DIVISION 1. GENERAL CORPORATION LAW [100 - 2319] ( Division 1 repealed and added by Stats. 1975, Ch. 682. ) CHAPTER 2. Organization and Bylaws [200 - 213] ( Chapter 2 added by Stats. 1975, Ch. 682. ) 201. (a) The Secretary of State shall not file articles setting forth a name in which “bank,” “ trust,” “trustee,” or related words appear, unless the certificate of approval of the Commissioner of Financial Protection and Innovation is attached thereto. This subdivision does not apply to the articles of any corporation subject to the Banking Law on which is endorsed the approval of the Commissioner of Financial Protection and Innovation. (b) The name of a corporation shall not be a name that the Secretary of State determines is likely to mislead the public and shall be distinguishable in the records of the Secretary of State from all of the following: (1) The name of any corporation. (2) The name of any foreign corporation authorized to transact intrastate business in this state. (3) Each name that is under reservation pursuant to this title. (4) The name of a foreign corporation that has registered its name pursuant to Section 2101. (5) An alternate name of a foreign corpora
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts the original text by weakening the obligation to comply with laws. The original text states "Each party shall comply with all prevailing laws..." while the changed text uses "Each party shall endeavor, to the extent practicable, to generally adhere to applicable laws..." Replacing 'shall comply' with 'shall endeavor...to generally adhere' introduces ambiguity and makes compliance discretionary, contradicting the original firm obligation. The Law Explanation states that making legal compliance discretionary undermines the legal basis of the agreement. Although Scraped Law Snippet 1 focuses on corporate naming conventions and doesn't directly address compliance obligations, the Perturbation Explanation clearly states that the modification introduces a discretionary element potentially leading to non-compliance with regulatory requirements, and the addition of the word 'generally' means that they do not have to comply with *all* laws, rules, and regulations.
